affected_groups: families reliant on free school choice
content.decision_question: Should Hungary pursue statutory delay of first tracking to age 14+ despite its low near-term political and legal feasibility, or concentrate on administratively deliverable but structurally partial reforms that leave the tracking mechanism itself intact?
content.evidence_boundary: Further evidence (e.g., piloting bundled curriculum/teacher-training reforms, tracking Finnish/Polish outcome trajectories over longer horizons) can clarify whether delay produces durable equity gains and what implementation sequencing reduces transition risk. It cannot resolve whether the value placed on preserving free choice and existing institutional structures should yield to equity gains, nor can it settle the ideological commitment to stratification as a policy goal.
content.tension: Comparative and equity evidence points to early tracking itself as the primary structural driver of inequality, favoring statutory delay (T3), yet delay requires overturning a constitutionally protected free-choice guarantee and legally entrenched track structure, faces an ideologically committed governing coalition, and confronts an acute implementation-capacity crisis (teacher shortfall, aging workforce). Meanwhile within-structure levers (T1, T2, T6) are administratively deliverable now but, by design, cannot reach the magnitude of change the structural evidence implies is necessary.
content.title: Structural delay versus incremental within-structure reform
